_note: Projected faction archetypes in a US instability scenario. Editorial scenario analysis, not prediction.

factions.name: Right Populists
factions.tagline: Anti-establishment nationalism rooted in cultural and economic grievance

base.demographics: Rural and exurban working/middle class, non-college white voters, veterans
base.geography: Heartland, Sun Belt, Appalachia, rural South and Midwest
base.institutions: Militia networks, evangelical churches, conservative media ecosystem, state legislatures

factorAlignment.description: Activated by deepening cultural polarization and eroding trust in federal institutions. Views institutional decay as evidence the system is beyond reform.

factions.wildcard: A charismatic leader who bridges the militia-mainstream gap could rapidly expand this faction's mobilization capacity beyond its current base.

factions.name: Progressive Left
factions.tagline: Anti-corporate movement demanding systemic economic restructuring

base.demographics: Urban younger voters, college-educated professionals, service workers, racial minorities
base.geography: Major metros, university towns, Pacific coast, Northeast corridor
base.institutions: Labor unions, activist networks, progressive media, university campuses, nonprofit organizations

factorAlignment.description: Driven by wealth concentration and economic precarity. Frames instability as a symptom of capitalism's structural failures and sees revolution as corrective, not destructive.

factions.wildcard: If a severe financial crisis hits, progressive demands for nationalization and wealth redistribution could move from fringe to mainstream overnight.

factions.name: Establishment Center
factions.tagline: Institutional loyalists from both parties defending the status quo

base.demographics: Professional-managerial class, suburban moderates, older voters, government employees
base.geography: Suburban rings, state capitals, Washington DC corridor, established metro areas
base.institutions: Federal bureaucracy, mainstream media, think tanks, corporate boards, both party establishments

factorAlignment.description: Stake in institutional continuity makes them the natural anti-revolution force. But declining trust in the very institutions they defend erodes their legitimacy and recruitment base.

factions.wildcard: A major institutional success (corruption prosecution, crisis competently managed) could restore enough trust to pull moderates back from the populist fringes.

factions.name: Libertarian Decentralists
factions.tagline: Tech-aligned anti-federalists seeking radical restructuring of governance

base.demographics: Tech workers, entrepreneurs, crypto community, younger libertarians, digital nomads
base.geography: Silicon Valley, Austin, Miami, remote/distributed networks, crypto hubs
base.institutions: Tech companies, DAOs, crypto exchanges, libertarian think tanks, seasteading movements

factorAlignment.description: Views economic disruption as proof that centralized systems fail. Doesn't want revolution per se, but rather wants the old system to collapse so decentralized alternatives can fill the vacuum.

factions.wildcard: If a major bank failure or currency crisis hits, crypto-native parallel financial systems could gain mass adoption, giving this faction disproportionate influence over economic infrastructure.

factions.name: Security Apparatus
factions.tagline: Military, law enforcement, and intelligence community: the kingmaker faction

base.demographics: Active military, veterans, law enforcement officers, intelligence community, National Guard
base.geography: Military bases nationwide, Pentagon corridor, Fort Liberty, Fort Hood, border regions
base.institutions: Department of Defense, FBI, DHS, state National Guard units, police unions, veteran organizations

factorAlignment.description: Directly confronts the consequences of rising protest intensity. Their response (suppress, stand down, or split) historically determines whether instability resolves or escalates into revolution.

factions.wildcard: A contested election or unconstitutional executive order that forces military leadership to choose sides could fracture this faction along officer/enlisted and federal/state lines, making it the single most destabilizing scenario.

factions.name: Corporate Oligarchy
factions.tagline: Billionaire class leveraging instability for consolidation

base.demographics: Ultra-wealthy individuals, corporate executives, hedge fund managers, media owners
base.geography: New York, San Francisco, and globally distributed, not bound by geography
base.institutions: Fortune 500, Wall Street, major media conglomerates, lobbying firms, private equity

factorAlignment.description: Both driver and beneficiary of inequality. Funds both sides of polarization through media ownership and campaign finance. Historically, oligarchic factions survive revolutions by switching allegiance to whichever side wins.

factions.wildcard: If key oligarchs publicly break with the establishment and fund populist movements, as some tech billionaires have already begun doing, it could accelerate instability dramatically.

factions.name: Labor Movement
factions.tagline: Working-class economic populism driven by union resurgence

base.demographics: Union workers, gig economy workers, service sector, nurses, teachers, warehouse workers
base.geography: Rust Belt, port cities, major distribution hubs, hospital-dense metros
base.institutions: AFL-CIO, SEIU, Teamsters, Amazon Labor Union, new-wave independent unions

factorAlignment.description: Activated by wage stagnation, job insecurity, and the widening gap between productivity and compensation. Historically, labor movements provide the mass mobilization base that other factions cannot generate alone.

factions.wildcard: A general strike, even a partial one affecting logistics, healthcare, or transportation, could shift the entire power dynamic. The Teamsters alone control 30% of US freight.

factions.name: Religious Traditionalists
factions.tagline: Christian nationalist movement anchored in cultural conservatism
factions.stanceLabel: Revolution If Culture War Demands It

base.demographics: Evangelical Christians, conservative Catholics, homeschool networks, rural religious communities
base.geography: Bible Belt, rural Midwest, exurban megachurch corridors
base.institutions: Megachurches, Christian media networks, faith-based schools, judicial advocacy groups (ADF, FRC)

factorAlignment.description: Perceives cultural changes as existential threat. Revolutionary impulse is conditional, activated when secular institutions are seen as hostile to religious values. Provides moral justification framework for other right-aligned factions.

factions.wildcard: A Supreme Court reversal on religious liberty issues, or perceived government persecution of churches, could transform this faction from passive cultural warriors into active revolutionary participants.
